author: valkare
content:
(Vote 4 sticky :3_chubby: )

Ok, I've gotten tired of reading and writing in a multitude of threads on how things like tuning should work between you, your fuel tuner, wideband, and trims.... So I'm going to write this long winded explanation to help people out and just refer noobs here. Before we start let's go over some terminology:

DISCLAIMER: I've been led to believe this is correct information through my various amounts of research. If someone has any advice/changes/corrections, let me know and I'll go through and edit the following to make it factually accurate. Thanks.

You car has two different ways of running the fuel system: Closed loop and Open loop

Closed loop – This is what the car is in at normal cruising, low throttle, idle, etc. This is where the ECU is running off your Long Term fuel trims (LTFT's, I'll describe these later), then reading your o2 sensors, and then making Short term fuel trim adjustments in an attempt to make the car operate at the stoich a/f ratio of 14.7. So while driving your car is constantly reading the o2's and fudging the STFT's to maintain 14.7.

Open loop – Your car is in open loop at a couple instances... for example when you mash the gas, it's in open loop, when you first turn your car on and the o2 sensors have not heated up to proper operating temperature, you're also in open loop. During this time the ECU justs runs off of the LTFT maps. It stops making STFT adjustments to achieve an a/f of 14.7 and whatever it has programmed in as a LTFT for a given RPM it will run that adjustment only. This makes tuning open loop very easy for this reason.

To determine when your car is in open or closed loop you can either take my word for it, or get an OBD-II scanner that outputs more information than just CELs. For example, my Actron 9145 tells me everything I want to know... fuel trims, fuel mode, etc. In my car, my system switches to open loop at about 40-45% throttle according to my Neo if it's worth anything.

Fuel trims: There are two kinds, Long term fuel trims (LTFT's) and short term fuel trims (STFT's). Let me preface by saying that when you have an OBD-II and can see these things work in real time it makes a lot more sense.

LTFT's – Think of these as a map of sorts. At a given RPM rate, say 3000 rpm's your car is attempting to achieve 14.7 a/f under normal cruising. Well after making some adjustments your car will either add fuel or subtract fuel from it's original fuel map to achieve this ratio. The long term adjustment that the ECU makes (say -11% or something) is what goes into your LTFT. These are merely a series of % adjustments that the ECU has made over time to calibrate to 14.7 based on what the o2's are telling the ECU.

STFT's – These are adjustments to the LTFT's during normal cruising to reach this 14.7 ratio. When you observe these in real time these fluctuate constantly while cruising around. For example, let's say at 3000 rpm's your car has a LTFT of -11% but your o2's are telling the ECU that the a/f is 13.0 (ie running a little rich) well the STFT's would then be say -5%... so your car will temporarily be trying to lean itself out by subtracting some fuel from the mixture. The way your car makes adjustments to the LTFT's is through the STFT's. If your STFT's keep hovering around say 10% @ X rpms, well then eventually it's going to transfer 10% to the LTFT's. The car's end goal is to have the STFT's be 0 and the a/f at 14.7. This way, all the adjustments are stored in the fuel map and the LTFT's.

Now onto tuning, for most people, tuning is simply something you do to a) maximize whp by achieving a certain a/f and b) to have a rich enough mixture to help keep it cool and prevent detonation (=bad). So let's talk about an n/a scenario first.

Each car is different and the optimal a/f ratio for max whp will be slightly different for everyone and their mods. But let's just say the optimal a/f for max whp for Joe Schmoe and his Tib is 13.5 a/f. Now Joe should really a) take his car to a dyno with his piggy-back tuner or b) read this thread, understand the in's and outs, and have a tuner and wideband. No exceptions. But ok, so Joe is ready to tune, and he wants to know what to tune and how to. Ok, since Joe is n/a he really should only be concerned with his Open Loop tuning (I'll explain why later). So the general process for Joe to street tune is to make a pull, record and make a chart of his a/f versus his rpm rate, and then make adjustments on his tuner. HE WILL MAKE ADJUSTMENTS IN HIS HIGH THROTTLE / OPEN LOOP MODE. On my neo for example, I have high throttle set for 50% (but you can set it wherever your car goes into open loop if you please). So Joe does this a couple times and keeps tinkering with his open loop settings at various rpms until he achieves his 13.5 ratio and is a happy camper.

The same general methodology holds true for tuning in general, you tinker with the open loop for when you're mashing the gas so you don't a) blow up the engine and b) can maximize the hp. Now let's say Joe didn't know the above and instead tuned his closed loop / low throttle mode. Now what happens? Well he's fine for a little bit... but as he starts driving normally at say 3000 rpms let's say Joe has an adjustment of -15%.... the car is seeing that it is running pretty lean at this point while cruising, and since Joe is subjecting his ECU and closed loop and trims to this -15% the ECU is going to start correcting itself.... Eventually Joe will find that his STFT's are consistently sitting around 15% (to counter his move) and eventually these will store themselves in addition to the existing LTFT. So say the LTFT was originally -3%, the STFT was 15% and Joe had a -15% fuel tuner adjustment... well after some cruising the LTFT is now 12%, Joe has -15%, and the STFT's will drift around 0% which in essence erases the good work Joe had done..... to some degree...... Joe's tune is still drifting around in the ECU and on his tuner, but the real problem is that when you push the ECU and fuel trims a bit too far the ECU gets upset... the trims can only move so far before your car will want to throw a CEL for running too lean or rich because once you exceed the threshold of the trims, your ECU will think something is wrong with your fuel or air system.

When should you be tuning closed loop? To really tune closed loop you will need an OBD-II scanner in addition to your tuner and wideband (most of the time, there is an exception). The exception being, let's say you put 290cc injectors into your car. Well your ecu think it's running 190cc's and you can see how tricking it will really be unpleasant. The solution is to across the board of RPM's tell your fuel tuner in closed loop mode to make an adjustment of -35%. By doing this you're now tricking the ECU into running 65% of the 290cc's which is roughly equal (close enough) to 190cc so the ECU won't throw a fit.

The other purpose of tuning closed loop is to preserve tunes. For example, when you disconnect your battery and reset your ECU you wipe out your fuel trims... now it takes the ECU a little while to learn everything again. So in an optimal world your closed loop tune on your piggyback would make the adjustments necessary to push your LTFT's to 0% and then your open loop tune would push you to the optimal a/f ratio for mashing the gas. So then basically what you're left with is the car running off of your piggyback's adjustments in closed loop (because you've accounted for all the LTFT's there), the STFT's will all fluctuate around 0% to keep you at 14.7 while cruising, and then when you mash the gas you'll end up with your high throttle tune kicking in and be at optimal a/f. Easy enough, right? The way to do all this is with an obd-ii and some normal cruising. What you'll need to do is after your ECU has learned all the LTFT's, take your car out for a cruise and make a chart of RPM vs. LTFT. Say at 2500rpms your LTFT is 5% then at 3000 its 7% and at 3500 its 10%... well then go to your tuner... set the closed loop tuning for 5% at 2.5k, 7% at 3k, and 10% at 3.5k.... and go cruise again. If you monitor your trims you'll see the STFT's have now mirrored your work in the tunes, as they should drift around -5%, -7%, and -10%.... eventually these will add to the existing LTFT's and make the LTFT's all 0 for the given entries.

That should about do it... I'll try to update and correct as more knowledgeable people step in and add their $0.02. Also, I know that you're not "adding or subtracting" fuel technically in your fuel tuner, but actually manipulating MAF signal... this is another issue that I really don't want to get in to at the moment, so another time perhaps.

But hope this helps everyone understand the in's and out's of what to do when tuning as this is what I've gathered over time through my research and understanding.
